title: What Would I Give if Generosity Always Returned?
subtitle: Journal prompts for examining what holds back giving and what moves freely
excerpt: Five journal prompts for looking at generosity as a two-way flow. The prompts start with where fear of giving shows up, then move to what would change if the return of every gift were certain.
sourceNotes: Adapted from the Day 58 reflection prompts in The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798276631448).

practice.whenToUse: Use when you want to understand what limits your generosity, or after a week of the giving gesture activity when you have fresh material to examine.

content.text: Five journal prompts for looking underneath generosity. The question 'what holds me back from giving freely' usually has a specific answer: a specific fear, a specific memory, a specific number below which giving feels dangerous. These prompts find that specific thing. Set twenty minutes and write without stopping.
New to journalling as practice? Read
When I give (money, time, or love), what part of me worries it won't return?
Write where the hesitation sits. Is it about money specifically, or about giving in general?
What is the story underneath the reluctance?
Write the belief plainly. 'I'll give and there won't be enough left.' 'Generosity is for people with more than me.' 'Giving is a loss unless I can afford it.' Write what it actually says.
If generosity always multiplied my abundance, what would I do differently today?
Write a specific action. Not 'I'd be more generous' but the actual thing you would do today.
Who or what could I give to this week that would remind me how abundant I already am?
Name someone or something specific. Write one small concrete gesture.
What has generosity already returned to me — in any form?
Look for examples where giving came back as something: money, opportunity, kindness, connection. Write three, however small.
Adapted from the Day 58 reflection prompts in The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
